title: Sorisa Is 17 and Already Outrunning Toronto's Hype Machine
subtitle: The Atlantic Records signee is making hyperpop that feels native to Toronto's restless energy — and he's barely old enough to drive.
author: Leelou Blanc
body:
[spotify:artist/5aHJ7mcBAI9x0Hv28IzNV8]

Toronto has a habit of producing artists who feel older than their years. Drake was already narrating his quarter-life crisis at 23. Majid Jordan were crafting R&B for people who had lived three lives by the time they hit 25. But Sorisa, born Sebastian Pena, currently 17 years old, is doing something different. He is not performing maturity. He is performing the chaos of being young right now, in real time, and making it sound like a genre that does not have a name yet. There is no retrospective quality to his music. It is happening now, to him, and you can hear the present tense of it.

[youtube:HOJ03R_O1sc]

## The Numbers Do Not Tell the Story

"U Look So Good in Fall" passed 600,000 streams and counting. He sold out Lee's Palace, a venue that has launched careers and ended egos in equal measure. Atlantic Records signed him. Rolling Loud Orlando has him on the May 2026 lineup. For a 17-year-old from Toronto, that is an extraordinary trajectory. But the numbers are the least interesting thing about Sorisa.

What matters is the sound. His music sits at the intersection of hyperpop's synthetic maximalism and Toronto's atmospheric melancholy, a combination that should not work but does, because Pena treats both traditions with genuine affection rather than ironic distance. There is no winking at the audience. No self-aware posturing about genre. He simply makes songs that move between euphoria and sadness with the kind of speed that feels honest to the way teenagers actually experience emotion. Fast is not a production gimmick. It is emotional realism.

## Toronto's Next Wave

The city has spent the last decade being defined by a very specific sound: moody, atmospheric R&B with OVO's fingerprints all over it. That sound is not dead, but it has calcified. The next generation, artists like Sorisa, are pulling from different wells entirely. Hyperpop's shattered production. Electropop's sugar rush. The emotional directness of emo rap without the nihilism. It is a sound that feels native to a generation raised on TikTok's 30-second attention economy but still craving something that lasts longer than a scroll.

His EP I Love Colours, released late last year, is the clearest statement so far. Five tracks that move fast and hit hard, each one exploring a different shade of the same restless energy. The production is bright and slightly abrasive in the best way, the musical equivalent of neon at 2 a.m.

The OVO comparison is worth examining because Sorisa is explicitly not in that lineage, which makes him interesting from a Toronto-music-history perspective. Drake's Toronto was defined by a particular relationship to melancholy, to nostalgia, to the weight of aspiration and what it costs. The emotional temperature of that music runs cool. Sorisa's music runs hot. Fast, bright, immediate, saturated with feeling that does not defer to irony. This is a different emotional contract between artist and audience, and it is reaching a generation of listeners who have had enough of cool. The distance of cool has been the Toronto default for long enough that its opposite now sounds fresh.

## The Production Approach

The production on I Love Colours is worth specific attention. The tracks are built around synthesizers that sit at the edge of overdriven, that carry a quality of slight distortion that feels deliberate rather than accidental. The drums are punchy and present in the mix, placed up front rather than buried, which gives the tracks a forward momentum that keeps them from settling into the atmospheric mode that Toronto production often defaults to.

The vocal treatment is also distinctive. Pena's voice is processed enough to feel part of the electronic texture but not so processed that the emotional content gets lost. This is a fine calibration and he gets it right. Hyperpop production at its laziest turns voices into instruments and loses the human quality that makes emotional content land. Sorisa keeps the human quality while still embedding the voice in the synthetic environment. The result feels both artificial and alive simultaneously, which is the exact quality that the best hyperpop achieves.

The mixing on "U Look So Good in Fall" in particular demonstrates a maturity that is impressive from a 17-year-old. The track manages to be dense without being cluttered, loud without being fatiguing, emotionally immediate without being simple. These are production qualities that many experienced producers do not reliably achieve. The track sounds finished in a specific sense: it knows what it is trying to do and it does that thing completely.

## Atlantic and What It Means

The Atlantic signing is both a marker of where he is and a set of questions about where he is going. Major label machinery can do extraordinary things for an artist's reach and can also smooth out the edges that made the artist worth signing. The rough edges on Sorisa's music, the slightly unpolished vocals, the production choices that lean toward messy rather than pristine, the willingness to be loud and bright in a city that has rewarded darkness and restraint for years, are not flaws. They are the source of what makes this music interesting.

Atlantic has the resources to push Sorisa into a million playlists. The question is whether they will let the rough edges stay. Right now those rough edges are the whole point. An over-produced Sorisa would be a different and lesser artist. The label has done right by young artists before. The hope is they understand what they signed.

## Why It Matters

There is always a risk with young artists signed to major labels early. Sorisa does not need to be the next Drake or the next anyone. Toronto is big enough for multiple traditions. What he needs is time, the one thing the industry is worst at giving young artists. At 17, with Lee's Palace already behind him and Rolling Loud ahead, the clock is running. But listen to the music and you hear someone who is already running faster.
